report_name: JDownloader Website Hacked to Distribute Python RAT in Supply Chain Attack

threat_actors.name: Unidentified JDownloader Attackers
threat_actors.description: Unidentified threat actors who compromised the official JDownloader website to distribute malware via a supply chain attack.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Supply Chain
attack_patterns.description: The attackers compromised the JDownloader website, a software distribution point, to replace legitimate download links with malicious ones.

attack_patterns.name: Python
attack_patterns.description: The malware payload delivered to Windows users was a Python-based Remote Access Trojan (RAT).

attack_patterns.name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
attack_patterns.description: The Python RAT payload for Windows was heavily obfuscated, implying it would need to decode itself at runtime to execute.

attack_patterns.name: Trap
attack_patterns.description: The Linux malware variant established persistence by creating a script in /etc/profile.d/systemd.sh, which is executed upon user login.

attack_patterns.name: Setuid and Setgid
attack_patterns.description: The Linux malware installed a SUID-root binary to maintain elevated privileges on the compromised system.

attack_patterns.name: Web Protocols
attack_patterns.description: The initial download of the malicious installers occurred over HTTP/HTTPS from the compromised JDownloader website and attacker-controlled servers.

attack_patterns.name: Delay Execution
attack_patterns.description: The Windows RAT used an eight-minute execution delay as a sandbox evasion technique before activating its primary payload.

attack_patterns.name: Match Legitimate Resource Name or Location
attack_patterns.description: The Linux malware masqueraded as a legitimate system process by using the name 'upowerd' and placing itself in '/usr/libexec/upowerd'.

malware.name: Python RAT (JDownloader)
malware.description: A heavily obfuscated Python-based Remote Access Trojan (RAT) delivered to Windows users who downloaded the compromised 'Alternative Installer' from the JDownloader website. It used an eight-minute execution delay to evade sandboxes.

malware.name: Linux Persistence ELF (JDownloader)
malware.description: Malicious ELF binaries delivered to Linux users. The malware established persistence via a script in /etc/profile.d and installed a SUID-root binary to maintain elevated privileges, while masquerading as the 'upowerd' process.

tools.name: JDownloader
tools.description: A popular open-source download manager whose official website was compromised and its installers trojanized to deliver malware in a supply chain attack.

tools.name: Pyarmor
tools.description: A likely tool used to heavily obfuscate the Python-based RAT, hindering analysis.

vulnerabilities.name: Unspecified JDownloader CMS Vulnerability
vulnerabilities.description: An unspecified, unpatched vulnerability in the JDownloader website's Content Management System (CMS) was exploited by attackers to gain access and modify download links.

identities.name: JDownloader
identities.description: A popular open-source download management tool whose official website was compromised in a supply chain attack between May 6 and May 7, 2026.

identities.name: AppWork GmbH
identities.description: The company that develops and maintains the JDownloader software.

infrastructure.name: JDownloader Malware Hosting Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: Attacker-controlled infrastructure used to host the malicious RAT and ELF binary payloads, linked from the compromised JDownloader website.

indicators.name: JDownloader Supply Chain Attack C2 Network IOCs
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: JDownloader Supply Chain Attack C2 Network Activity
id: 2a9b3c4d-e5f6-4a7b-8c9d-0e1f2a3b4c5d
status: experimental
description: Detects network traffic to domains and URLs known to host malware payloads from the JDownloader supply chain attack in May 2026.
references:
  - https://securityaffairs.co/wordpress/162985/malware/jdownloader-site-malware-supply-chain-attack.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-10
tags:
  - attack.t1195.002
  - attack.command_and_control
level: high
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    c-uri|contains:
      - 'parkspringshotel.com/m/Lu6aeloo.php'
      - 'auraguest.lk/m/douV2quu.php'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects network connections to malicious URLs and domains used to distribute malware in the JDownloader supply chain attack.

indicators.name: JDownloader Linux Malware Persistence File Creation
indicators.pattern:
title: JDownloader Linux Malware Persistence via profile.d
id: 8d7c6b5a-4f3e-4d2c-1b0a-987654321fed
status: experimental
description: Detects the creation of a malicious shell script in /etc/profile.d, a technique used by the Linux variant of the JDownloader malware for persistence.
references:
  - https://securityaffairs.co/wordpress/162985/malware/jdownloader-site-malware-supply-chain-attack.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-10
tags:
  - attack.t1546.004
  - attack.persistence
level: high
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: linux
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename: '/etc/profile.d/systemd.sh'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the creation of the `/etc/profile.d/systemd.sh` file, a strong indicator of compromise by the Linux malware variant.

indicators.name: JDownloader Linux Malware Masquerading Process
indicators.pattern:
title: JDownloader Linux Malware Masquerading as upowerd
id: 1e2f3a4b-5c6d-7e8f-9a0b-1c2d3e4f5a6b
status: experimental
description: 'Detects a process named upowerd running from a non-standard location (/usr/libexec/upowerd), a masquerading technique used by the Linux malware from the JDownloader compromise.'
references:
  - https://securityaffairs.co/wordpress/162985/malware/jdownloader-site-malware-supply-chain-attack.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-10
tags:
  - attack.t1036.005
  - attack.defense_evasion
level: high
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: linux
detection:
  selection:
    Image: '/usr/libexec/upowerd'
  filter_legit:
    ParentImage: '/usr/lib/systemd/systemd'
  condition: selection and not filter_legit
indicators.description: Detects the execution of a process named `upowerd` from the non-standard location `/usr/libexec/upowerd`, indicating malware masquerading.

relationships.target_name: Match Legitimate Resource Name or Location
